title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: Every irtsim sample-size recommendation rests item parameters hand irt_design(). Discrimination () difficulty (b) values determine test-information curve, turn determines quickly criterion (mean squared error, bias, coverage, …) tightens N grows. Wrong parameters produce wrong code — produce plausible-looking N small large test actually plan administer. getting-started vignette (vignette("irtsim")) sketches three ways specify parameters: hand, via helper, prior fit. vignette deeper reference applied users need worked example path plus reference table typical values common assessment domains.
code: library(irtsim)

what: Path A — Import from a prior fit
title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: already calibrated instrument (similar one), fastest planning input parameter estimates. Two common cases: saved mirt model object, parameter table CSV / Excel file.

previous_headings: Path A — Import from a prior fit
what: A1. From a mirt fit object
title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: mirt::coef(fit, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)$items returns matrix IRT parameterization irt_design() expects. 2PL fit columns , b, g (guessing, 0 2PL), u (upper, 1 2PL); pull b done. saved fit slope-intercept (d) values. older mirt workflows store coef(fit, IRTpars = FALSE) output, gives a1 d rather b. Convert standard identity b = -d / : graded-response (GRM) fits, b columns category thresholds (b1, b2, …). Pass matrix irt_design(model = "GRM", item_params = list(= ..., b = ...)).
code: prior_data <- mirt::expand.table(mirt::LSAT7) prior_fit  <- mirt::mirt(prior_data, 1, "2PL", verbose = FALSE)  co <- mirt::coef(prior_fit, IRTpars = TRUE, simplify = TRUE)$items co #>                a          b g u #> Item.1 0.9879254 -1.8787456 0 1 #> Item.2 1.0808847 -0.7475160 0 1 #> Item.3 1.7058006 -1.0576962 0 1 #> Item.4 0.7651853 -0.6351358 0 1 #> Item.5 0.7357980 -2.5204102 0 1  design_from_fit <- irt_design(   model       = "2PL",   n_items     = nrow(co),   item_params = list(a = co[, "a"], b = co[, "b"]) ) design_from_fit #> IRT Design #>   Model:        2PL  #>   Items:        5 items #>   Theta dist:   normal  #>   Factors:      1  #>   a range:      [0.736, 1.706] #>   b range:      [-2.52, -0.635] co_si <- mirt::coef(prior_fit, IRTpars = FALSE, simplify = TRUE)$items a_vec <- co_si[, "a1"] b_vec <- -co_si[, "d"] / a_vec all.equal(b_vec, co[, "b"])  # same as IRTpars=TRUE path #> [1] TRUE

what: A2. From a CSV or Excel parameter table
title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: Technical manuals prior calibration reports often publish parameter tables CSV Excel. Read reshape list form irt_design() expects. Excel, swap read.csv() readxl::read_excel() (preferred reader); rest pattern identical. contract irt_design() enforces b numeric vectors length n_items (matrix b GRM).
code: # Imagine this CSV came from a prior calibration report. csv_text <- " item,a,b i01,1.05,-1.80 i02,0.92,-0.95 i03,1.18,-0.20 i04,1.40, 0.45 i05,0.88, 1.15 i06,1.22, 1.95 " params_df <- read.csv(text = csv_text, strip.white = TRUE)  design_from_csv <- irt_design(   model       = "2PL",   n_items     = nrow(params_df),   item_params = list(a = params_df$a, b = params_df$b) ) design_from_csv #> IRT Design #>   Model:        2PL  #>   Items:        6 items #>   Theta dist:   normal  #>   Factors:      1  #>   a range:      [0.88, 1.4] #>   b range:      [-1.8, 1.95]

title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: prior fit planning brand-new test, next-best input distribution drawn values typical assessment domain. reference table summarises four common domains; calls follow show instantiate irt_params_2pl() (irt_params_grm() polytomous clinical scales). worked example per domain — n_items = 20, distinct distribution arguments: list goes straight irt_design():
code: # Cognitive ability — high discriminations, broad difficulty range ip_cog <- irt_params_2pl(   n_items = 20,   a_mean  = 0.20, a_sd  = 0.30,   # log-normal: median a ~ 1.22   b_mean  = 0,    b_sd  = 1.20,   seed    = 1 ) summary(ip_cog$a); summary(ip_cog$b) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>  0.6285  1.0893  1.3606  1.3372  1.5338  1.9711 #>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max.  #> -2.387222 -0.479359 -0.065960 -0.007766  0.786796  1.630415 # Personality — moderate discriminations, broader trait coverage ip_pers <- irt_params_2pl(   n_items = 20,   a_mean  = -0.20, a_sd = 0.30,   # log-normal: median a ~ 0.82   b_mean  = 0,     b_sd = 1.50,   seed    = 1 ) summary(ip_pers$a); summary(ip_pers$b) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>  0.4213  0.7302  0.9121  0.8964  1.0282  1.3213 #>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max.  #> -2.984028 -0.599199 -0.082450 -0.009707  0.983495  2.038019 # Clinical (PROMIS-style GRM, 5 categories) — high discriminations, # trait-spanning thresholds ip_clin <- irt_params_grm(   n_items      = 20,   n_categories = 5,   a_mean       = 0.10, a_sd = 0.30,   # median a ~ 1.10   b_mean       = 0,    b_sd = 1.20,   seed         = 1 ) summary(ip_clin$a) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>  0.5687  0.9856  1.2311  1.2100  1.3879  1.7835 # Achievement / large-scale educational — moderate discriminations, # difficulty distribution centered on the cut score (here 0) ip_ach <- irt_params_2pl(   n_items = 20,   a_mean  = 0,    a_sd  = 0.25,   # median a = 1.00   b_mean  = 0,    b_sd  = 1.00,   seed    = 1 ) summary(ip_ach$a); summary(ip_ach$b) #>    Min. 1st Qu.  Median    Mean 3rd Qu.    Max.  #>  0.5748  0.9089  1.0941  1.0737  1.2090  1.4901 #>      Min.   1st Qu.    Median      Mean   3rd Qu.      Max.  #> -1.989352 -0.399466 -0.054967 -0.006472  0.655663  1.358680 design_cog <- irt_design(model = "2PL", n_items = 20, item_params = ip_cog) design_cog #> IRT Design #>   Model:        2PL  #>   Items:        20 items #>   Theta dist:   normal  #>   Factors:      1  #>   a range:      [0.629, 1.971] #>   b range:      [-2.387, 1.63]

title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: neither prior fit tight domain prior, still content knowledge: item-review judgements, target trait range, expected pass rates. Translate distribution arguments. worked example. Suppose planning 12-item screener narrow construct. Content review suggests: Items target mid--upper trait range (respondents pass easiest items, hardest items separate high end). Translation: b_dist = "even", b_range = c(-0.5, 2). Items came single SME team consistent quality; expect moderate, narrow-spread discriminations. Translation: a_mean = 0, a_sd = 0.20 (median = 1, narrow lognormal). trick keep translation explicit: distribution argument map back content-review statement can defend. justify b_sd = 1.5 b_sd = 1.0, run sample-size simulation report conservative recommendation.

title: Choosing Item Parameters for Sample-Size Planning
text: Typical parameter ranges four common assessment domains. starting points, standards — confirm instrument’s technical manual published calibration domain locking planning N. Caveats. (1) Ranges rules thumb drawn cited literature; exact values vary population, content domain, calibration sample. (2) 3PL guessing parameters (relevant multiple-choice cognitive items) upper-asymptote (4PL) yet supported irtsim — planned v0.2.0. (3) unsure, simulate optimistic pessimistic parameter assumption report conservative N.

title: Planning a Sample Size for an IRT Study
text: planning item response theory (IRT) study need answer one question: many examinees need? Power-analysis formulas exist simple designs, real assessments combine multiple items, multiple parameters per item, missing-data mechanisms, — increasingly — model misspecification fully characterize priori. irtsim answers question simulation: specify plausible data-generating model, sweep across candidate sample sizes, fit estimation model many times, report sample size chosen performance criterion (mean squared error, bias, coverage, …) crosses target threshold. package implements 10-decision framework Schroeders & Gnambs (2025). vignette walks abridged version: pick design, pick sample sizes, run, interpret.
code: library(irtsim) library(ggplot2)

title: Planning a Sample Size for an IRT Study
text: irt_study() adds things vary across simulation grid: sample sizes want compare, optionally missing-data mechanism, optionally estimation model differs data-generating model (model misspecification studies). -missing-data planning question, two arguments enough. four sample sizes span typical planning range: 100 small 10-item 2PL, 1000 ample. simulation give us curve .
code: study <- irt_study(   design       = design_helper,   sample_sizes = c(100, 250, 500, 1000) ) study #> IRT Study #>   Model:          2PL  #>   Items:          10  #>   Sample sizes:   100, 250, 500, 1000  #>   Missing data:   none (complete data)

title: Planning a Sample Size for an IRT Study
text: irt_simulate() work happens: (sample_size, iteration) cell, generates data design, fits estimation model, stores parameter estimates. Two arguments control runtime: iterations (iterations → tighter Monte Carlo standard errors, longer runtime) parallel (default; turn production-scale runs). fixed seed required — every simulation reproducible default. progress = FALSE suppresses cli progress bar vignette renders cleanly. real studies, leave . iterations = 50 small — chosen keep vignette build fast. production planning use 500–1000 iterations (use irt_iterations() compute count needed target Monte Carlo standard error). Set parallel = TRUE dispatch iterations across future workers; reproducibility preserved within mode.
code: results <- irt_simulate(   study      = study,   iterations = 50,   seed       = 1,   progress   = FALSE )

title: Planning a Sample Size for an IRT Study
text: summary() returns one row per (sample_size, item, parameter) combination, performance criteria attached. Plot criterion interest sample size see curve flattens. line one item’s b (difficulty) MSE trajectory. dashed horizontal line planning threshold (0.05 — common default parameter recovery). Items whose lines cross threshold adequately recovered sample size; items still need larger N.

title: Planning a Sample Size for an IRT Study
text: recommended_n() reads smallest sample size criterion crosses threshold. default rolls per-item recommendations single number (maximum, item left -powered): scalar return headline answer. details attribute preserves per-item table can inspect items drove recommendation: want less conservative summary, pass aggregate = "mean" aggregate = "median". get legacy per-item data frame back, use aggregate = "none".
code: n_rec <- recommended_n(res_summary,                        criterion = "mse",                        threshold = 0.05,                        param     = "b") #> Warning: No tested sample size meets mse <= 0.05 for some item/param combinations. #> ℹ Affected: (item 5, param b) and (item 6, param b) #> ℹ Aggregate returned as NA. Inspect `attr(result, "details")` for per-item #>   values. n_rec #> [1] NA #> attr(,"details") #>    item param recommended_n criterion threshold #> 1     1     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 2     2     b           500       mse      0.05 #> 3     3     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 4     4     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 5     5     b            NA       mse      0.05 #> 6     6     b            NA       mse      0.05 #> 7     7     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 8     8     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 9     9     b           500       mse      0.05 #> 10   10     b           250       mse      0.05 #> attr(,"aggregate") #> [1] "max" #> attr(,"criterion") #> [1] "mse" #> attr(,"threshold") #> [1] 0.05 attr(n_rec, "details") #>    item param recommended_n criterion threshold #> 1     1     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 2     2     b           500       mse      0.05 #> 3     3     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 4     4     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 5     5     b            NA       mse      0.05 #> 6     6     b            NA       mse      0.05 #> 7     7     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 8     8     b           250       mse      0.05 #> 9     9     b           500       mse      0.05 #> 10   10     b           250       mse      0.05

what: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
title: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
text: Given vector per-iteration estimates true parameter value, computes bias, empirical SE, MSE, RMSE, coverage, Monte Carlo SEs following Morris et al. (2019).

title: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
code: compute_criterion(estimates, true_value, ci_lower = NULL, ci_upper = NULL)

title: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
text: estimates Numeric vector per-iteration parameter estimates. May contain NAs (non-converged iterations), excluded. true_value Single numeric value. data-generating (true) parameter value. ci_lower Optional numeric vector (length estimates). Lower bounds confidence intervals. NULL, coverage computed. ci_upper Optional numeric vector (length estimates). Upper bounds confidence intervals. NULL, coverage computed.

title: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
text: named list elements: bias Mean estimate minus true value. empirical_se Sample standard deviation estimates (n-1 denominator). mse Mean squared error: mean((estimate - true_value)^2). rmse Root mean squared error: sqrt(mse). coverage Proportion CIs containing true value, NULL CIs provided. NAs CIs excluded denominator. mcse_bias Monte Carlo SE bias: empirical_se / sqrt(K). mcse_mse Monte Carlo SE MSE: sd((est - true)^2) / sqrt(K).

title: Compute Performance Criteria for a Single Parameter (Internal) — compute_criterion
text: Morris, T. P., White, . R., & Crowther, M. J. (2019). Using simulation studies evaluate statistical methods. Statistics Medicine, 38(11), 2074–2102. doi:10.1002/sim.8086

what: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
title: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
text: Define data-generating model IRT simulation study. captures decisions 1–3 Schroeders & Gnambs (2025) framework: dimensionality, item parameters, item type.

title: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
code: irt_design(model, n_items, item_params, theta_dist = "normal", n_factors = 1L)

title: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
text: model Character string specifying IRT model. One "1PL", "2PL", "GRM". n_items Positive integer. Number items instrument. item_params named list item parameters. Contents depend model: 1PL b (numeric vector length n_items). Discrimination fixed 1 items added automatically. 2PL (discrimination, positive numeric vector matrix) b (difficulty, numeric vector), length n_items. GRM (discrimination, positive numeric vector) length n_items b (threshold matrix, n_items rows n_categories - 1 columns). theta_dist Either character string ("normal" "uniform") function takes single argument n returns numeric vector length n. Defaults "normal". n_factors Positive integer specifying number latent factors. Defaults 1L. Currently n_factors = 1 supported; multidimensional IRT (n_factors > 1) planned v0.4.0. Passing value 1 raises error rather silently propagating unsupported design estimator.

title: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
text: S3 object class irt_design (named list) elements model, n_items, item_params, theta_dist, n_factors.

title: Create an IRT Design Specification — irt_design
code: # 1PL (Rasch) design with 20 items design_1pl <- irt_design(   model = "1PL",   n_items = 20,   item_params = list(b = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 20)) )  # 2PL design design_2pl <- irt_design(   model = "2PL",   n_items = 30,   item_params = list(     a = rlnorm(30, 0, 0.25),     b = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 30)   ) )

what: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
text: Uses Burton (2003) formula determine minimum number simulation replications needed achieve desired level Monte Carlo precision.

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
code: irt_iterations(sigma, delta, alpha = 0.05)

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
text: sigma Positive numeric. empirical standard error estimand across replications (pilot estimate thereof). delta Positive numeric. acceptable Monte Carlo error (half-width MC confidence interval estimand). alpha Numeric (0, 1). Two-sided significance level. Default 0.05 (.e., 95 percent MC confidence).

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
text: integer: minimum number replications.

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
text: formula : $$R = \lceil (z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \sigma / \delta)^2 \rceil$$ \(\sigma\) empirical standard error estimand, \(\delta\) acceptable Monte Carlo error, \(z_{\alpha/2}\) critical value desired confidence level.

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
text: Burton, ., Altman, D. G., Royston, P., & Holder, R. L. (2006). design simulation studies medical statistics. Statistics Medicine, 25(24), 4279–4292. doi:10.1002/sim.2673

title: Compute Required Monte Carlo Replications — irt_iterations
code: # How many replications for MC SE of bias < 0.1 # when empirical SE of the estimand is 0.5? irt_iterations(sigma = 0.5, delta = 0.1) #> [1] 97  # Tighter tolerance with 99% MC confidence irt_iterations(sigma = 0.5, delta = 0.05, alpha = 0.01) #> [1] 664

what: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: Execute Monte Carlo simulation study based irt_study specification. iteration sample size, data generated, missing values applied, IRT model fitted, parameter estimates extracted stored.

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
code: irt_simulate(   study,   iterations,   seed,   progress = TRUE,   parallel = FALSE,   se = TRUE,   compute_theta = TRUE )

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: study irt_study object specifying design study conditions. iterations Positive integer. Number Monte Carlo replications. seed Integer. Base random seed reproducibility. iteration uses seed + iteration - 1. progress Logical. Print progress messages? Default TRUE. parallel Logical. Run iterations parallel using future.apply::future_lapply()? Default FALSE. Requires users set future plan (e.g., future::plan(multisession)) calling. See Details. se Logical. Compute standard errors confidence intervals item parameter estimates? Default TRUE. Set FALSE significant speed improvement point estimates needed (e.g., MSE, bias, RMSE criteria). FALSE, se/ci_lower/ci_upper columns item_results NA. compute_theta Logical. Compute EAP theta estimates recovery metrics (correlation, RMSE)? Default TRUE. Set FALSE skip mirt::fscores() call theta recovery needed. FALSE, theta_cor theta_rmse theta_results NA (converged still tracked).

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: S3 object class irt_results containing: item_results Data frame per-iteration item parameter estimates (columns: iteration, sample_size, item, param, true_value, estimate, se, ci_lower, ci_upper, converged). theta_results Data frame per-iteration theta recovery summaries (columns: iteration, sample_size, theta_cor, theta_rmse, converged). study original irt_study object. iterations Number replications run. seed Base seed used. elapsed Elapsed wall-clock time seconds. se Logical flag indicating whether SEs CIs computed. compute_theta Logical flag indicating whether theta recovery metrics computed.

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: returned irt_results object stores raw per-iteration estimates. Use summary.irt_results() compute performance criteria (bias, MSE, RMSE, coverage, etc.) plot.irt_results() visualize results.

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: parallel = TRUE, Monte Carlo loop iterations parallelized via future.apply::future_lapply(). parallel task processes one iteration across sample sizes sequentially. Important: function configure future plan. Users must set plan calling parallel = TRUE:   Without explicit plan, future defaults sequential execution (parallelism).
code: library(future) plan(multisession, workers = 4)  # or your preferred backend results <- irt_simulate(study, iterations = 100, seed = 42, parallel = TRUE)

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate
text: Reproducibility guaranteed within given dispatch mode, across modes: Serial mode (parallel = FALSE) uses deterministic per-cell seeds session's default RNG kind (Mersenne-Twister). Re-running base seed reproduces identical results bit--bit. Parallel mode (parallel = TRUE) delegates RNG management future.apply::future_lapply(..., future.seed = TRUE), assigns iteration formally independent L'Ecuyer-CMRG substream. Re-running base seed reproduces identical results bit--bit across parallel runs, including across different worker counts. Across modes, numerical results differ two paths use different RNG algorithms different seeding strategies. statistically valid; parallel path stronger formal guarantee independent substreams, standard Monte Carlo work. Progress messages suppressed parallel mode (workers stream stdout safely). Set progress = FALSE serial mode suppress messages (appear every 10% iterations).

title: Run an IRT Monte Carlo Simulation — irt_simulate

title: Define Study Conditions for an IRT Simulation — irt_study

title: Define Study Conditions for an IRT Simulation — irt_study
code: irt_study(   design,   sample_sizes,   missing = "none",   missing_rate = NULL,   test_design = NULL,   estimation_model = NULL )

title: Define Study Conditions for an IRT Simulation — irt_study
text: design irt_design object specifying data-generating model. sample_sizes Integer vector sample sizes evaluate. Values coerced integer, sorted ascending order, deduplicated. missing Character string specifying missing data mechanism. One "none" (default), "mcar", "mar", "booklet", "linking". missing_rate Numeric value \([0, 1)\) specifying proportion missing data. Required missing "mcar" "mar"; ignored missing "none". test_design list specifying test design structured missingness. Required missing "booklet" "linking". booklet Must contain booklet_matrix: binary matrix (n_booklets x n_items) 1 indicates item administered. linking Must contain linking_matrix: binary matrix (n_forms x n_items) 1 indicates item appears form. estimation_model Character string specifying IRT model fit. One "1PL", "2PL", "GRM". NULL (default), defaults design$model (.e., generation model also estimation model). Set different model perform misspecification studies (e.g., generate 2PL, estimate 1PL). Cross-fits allowed within response format (binary: 1PL, 2PL; polytomous: GRM).

title: Define Study Conditions for an IRT Simulation — irt_study
text: S3 object class irt_study (named list) elements design, missing, missing_rate, sample_sizes, test_design, estimation_model.

title: Define Study Conditions for an IRT Simulation — irt_study
code: # Simple study with no missing data d <- irt_design(   model = "1PL", n_items = 20,   item_params = list(b = seq(-2, 2, length.out = 20)) ) study <- irt_study(d, sample_sizes = c(100, 250, 500))  # Study with MCAR missingness study_mcar <- irt_study(d, sample_sizes = c(200, 400),                         missing = "mcar", missing_rate = 0.2)  # Model misspecification: generate 2PL, fit 1PL d_2pl <- irt_design(   model = "2PL", n_items = 15,   item_params = list(a = rlnorm(15, 0, 0.25), b = rnorm(15)) ) study_misspec <- irt_study(d_2pl, sample_sizes = c(100, 300),                            estimation_model = "1PL")

what: irtsim: Monte Carlo Simulation-Based Sample-Size Planning for Item Response Theory — irtsim-package
title: irtsim: Monte Carlo Simulation-Based Sample-Size Planning for Item Response Theory — irtsim-package
text: Provides pipeline application programming interface (API) Monte Carlo simulation-based sample-size planning item response theory (IRT). Implements 10-decision framework Schroeders Gnambs (2025) doi:10.1177/25152459251314798  three-step workflow: specify data-generating model irt_design(), add study conditions irt_study(), run simulations irt_simulate(). Supports one-parameter logistic (1PL), two-parameter logistic (2PL), graded response models missing-completely--random (MCAR), missing--random (MAR), booklet, linking missingness mechanisms. Results include mean squared error (MSE), bias, root mean squared error (RMSE), standard error (SE), coverage criteria summary plot methods.

title: irtsim: Monte Carlo Simulation-Based Sample-Size Planning for Item Response Theory — irtsim-package
text: Maintainer: Stephen Ward stephen_ward+irtsim@abhome.co

title: Summarize IRT Simulation Results — summary.irt_results
text: Compute performance criteria sample size, item, parameter combination irt_simulate() result. Criteria follow Morris et al. (2019) definitions. Optionally, users can provide custom callback function compute additional item-level performance criteria (e.g., conditional reliability, external criterion SE).

title: Summarize IRT Simulation Results — summary.irt_results

title: Summarize IRT Simulation Results — summary.irt_results
text: object irt_results object irt_simulate(). criterion Optional character vector. criteria include output. Valid values: "bias", "empirical_se", "mse", "rmse", "coverage", "mcse_bias", "mcse_mse". NULL (default), criteria returned. param Optional character vector. parameter types include (e.g., "", "b", "b1"). NULL (default), parameters summarized. criterion_fn Optional function. user-defined callback compute custom performance criteria. Must accept named arguments estimates (numeric vector), true_value (scalar), ci_lower (numeric), ci_upper (numeric), converged (logical), ... (future use). Must return named numeric vector length >= 1. names become new columns item_summary, appended n_converged. NULL (default), custom criteria computed. ... Additional arguments (ignored).

title: Summarize IRT Simulation Results — summary.irt_results

title: irtsim 0.1.2
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-05 New choosing-item-parameters vignette (vignette("choosing-item-parameters")) — deeper reference three item-parameter specification workflows introduced getting-started vignette: import prior mirt fit (slope-intercept--IRT conversion worked example) CSV / Excel parameter table; domain-typical preset values cognitive ability, personality, clinical, achievement assessments cited reference ranges; hypothesized / content-based specification explicit translation item-review judgements distribution arguments. eval=TRUE knitr engine; new exported helpers release. New irtsim getting-started vignette (vignette("irtsim")) walking stranger “need plan IRT study” recommended_n(). vignette builds live (eval=TRUE) drift package API. Three item-parameter specification paths demonstrated: hand, via irt_params_2pl(), prior mirt calibration. irt_design() now aborts informative error n_factors != 1. Multidimensional IRT support planned v0.4.0; , parameter retained design forward compatibility silently accepting n_factors > 1 produced cryptic mirt internal error downstream. new abort fires front points users planned support. recommended_n() gains aggregate parameter ("max" / "mean" / "median" / "none", default "max"). default return now integer scalar — smallest sample size powers every item/param requested threshold — details attribute carrying per-item data frame plus aggregate, criterion, threshold attributes. "mean" "median" round via ceiling() recommendation never falls central tendency. Pass aggregate = "none" recover previous per-item data frame return. Behavior change: default return shape changed per-item data frame scalar; closes footgun users -power forgetting take max() across items. Removed paper-reproduction-gaps vignette. content scorecard paper Examples 2 3 reproduction gaps pointed deferred objectives (Obj 30/31). objectives now superseded planned pluggable fit_fn / extract_fn hook (Obj 39, targeted v0.3.0); standalone gaps vignette longer reflects roadmap. Cross-references paper-example-2-mcar paper-example-3-grm also removed.

title: irtsim 0.1.0
text: irt_design() specifies data-generating IRT model (items, parameters, theta distribution). irt_study() adds study conditions (sample sizes, missing-data mechanism, optional separate estimation model). irt_simulate() runs Monte Carlo simulation loop deterministic seeding optional parallelism. summary(), plot(), recommended_n() methods extract simulation-based sample-size recommendations irt_results objects.

text: irt_study(estimation_model = ...) allows fitting different IRT model one used generate data (e.g., generate 2PL, fit 1PL). Compatible cross-pairs: (1PL, 2PL), (2PL, 1PL), -model. GRM cross-compatible dichotomous models.

title: irtsim 0.1.0
text: irt_simulate(parallel = TRUE) dispatches iterations across workers via future.apply::future_lapply(). Reproducibility contract: within-mode (identical results re-run given parallel setting) guaranteed. Cross-mode results differ serial uses Mersenne-Twister parallel uses L’Ecuyer-CMRG substreams — statistically valid. Users control backend via future::plan().
